questions.question: Which of the following backup terms does not fit with the others?
A view built from two or more different tables can have which of the following properties?
options: Can be deleted without affecting underlying data

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the SQL CASCADE option in a foreign key constraint?
options: Restricts changes in the parent table
Creates a backup of the database
Automatically generates unique values for the foreign key
Updates or deletes related records in child tables when changes occur in the parent table

questions.question: Which SQL statement is used to add a new column to an existing table?
What does CRUD stand for in the context of database operations?
What does ACID stand for in the context of database transactions?
What is normalization in database design?
options: Reducing redundancy and dependency by organizing fields and tables
Adding unnecessary fields for future use

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a data warehouse?
options: A database optimized for online transaction processing (OLTP)
A database optimized for analysis and reporting of large volumes of data
A database used for temporary storage of data
A database used for real-time data processing

questions.question: What does the term "OLAP" stand for in the context of databases?
What is a composite key in a database?
options: A key that consists of multiple attributes
A key that is generated automatically by the system
A key that is unique across all tables in the database
A key that is used for sorting records

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a NoSQL database?
What does the term "SQL injection" refer to?
options: A technique for optimizing SQL queries
A security vulnerability that allows attackers to execute malicious SQL code
A method for encrypting SQL data
A mechanism for creating temporary SQL tables

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of NoSQL databases?
Which of the following is NOT a type of database constraint?
What does the term "database schema" represent?
options: A physical storage location for database files
A predefined set of rules governing data integrity
The structure that defines the organization of data in a database
A mechanism for restricting access to certain database objects

questions.question: What is the typical workload pattern in OLTP systems?
options: Highly variable with peaks and troughs
Intermittent with long periods of inactivity

questions.question: What is the purpose of using table aliases in SQL joins?
options: To limit the number of rows returned by the join operation
To enforce referential integrity constraints between joined tables
To avoid ambiguity when referencing columns from multiple tables
To improve query performance by optimizing the join algorithm

questions.question: Which of the following operations is typically performed using DML?
What does the acronym BCNF stand for in terms of database normalization?
What is the key constraint for the fourth normalization form?
options: It requires every determinant to be a candidate key
It ensures no multiple independent multi-valued facts about an entity in a record

questions.question: What is a transitive functional dependency?
options: Changing a non-key column might cause another non-key column to change
When there exists more than one candidate key
It ensures rows in one table have corresponding rows in another
A primary key is functional dependent on a subset of candidate key relationships

questions.question: In a multi-user database system, what does the term "concurrency control" refer to?
options: The ability to process transactions independently of each other
The ability to ensure that transactions are processed as a single unit of work
The ability to ensure that changes made by one transaction do not interfere with changes made by other transactions
The ability to maintain the consistency of the database before and after a transaction

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a transitive dependency?
options: A student's ID, course, and instructor

questions.question: What is a "dirty read" in the context of database transactions?
options: A read operation that retrieves data from the database without applying any locks
A read operation that retrieves uncommitted data changes made by another transaction
A read operation that retrieves inconsistent data due to a database corruption
A read operation that retrieves data from a table without any specified criteria

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a level of isolation defined by the SQL standard?
In which isolation level are dirty reads allowed in most database systems?
Given user A reads a record and user B inserts or deleted rows to A’s record. If user A reads the same rows again, a new row is found. This is known as what?
Which database locking mechanism allows multiple transactions to read a data item simultaneously but prevents any transaction from writing to it?
What is an advantage of using optimistic locking over pessimistic locking?
In which scenario would you choose to use a stored procedure instead of an ad-hoc SQL query executed from an application?
options: When the SQL query needs to be executed only once
When the SQL query needs to be executed multiple times with different parameters
When the SQL query involves complex business logic or multiple steps
When the SQL query requires dynamic table creation or alteration

questions.question: How can stored procedures improve database performance compared to executing ad-hoc SQL queries from an application?
options: Stored procedures cache execution plans, reducing query compilation overhead
Stored procedures enforce stronger data consistency rules
Stored procedures automatically optimize SQL statements for better performance
Stored procedures eliminate the need for indexes on database tables

questions.question: What is the difference between a stored procedure and a user-defined function (UDF) in a database?
options: Stored procedures can only return scalar values, whereas UDFs can return result sets
Stored procedures can execute DML statements, whereas UDFs cannot
Stored procedures can only be invoked by the database administrator, whereas UDFs can be invoked by any user
Stored procedures are precompiled and stored in the database, whereas UDFs are compiled at runtime

questions.question: Which of the following statements accurately describes dynamic SQL within a stored procedure?
options: Dynamic SQL allows stored procedures to be executed without compilation
Dynamic SQL is a feature that enables the creation of temporary tables within a stored procedure
Dynamic SQL allows stored procedures to construct and execute SQL statements at runtime
Dynamic SQL provides built-in error handling mechanisms for stored procedures

questions.question: A key that is invented or made up for the sole purpose of being used as the primary key is known as what type of key?
Which of the following operations is NOT typically performed using DDL?
In a database ER diagram, what does a solid line connecting two entity sets represent?
What does DBaaS stand for?
Which of the following is a typical use case for DBaaS?
What is a key benefit of using a managed DBaaS solution?
options: Increased need for in-house database expertise

questions.question: What is polyglot persistence?
options: Storing data in multiple languages for internationalization purposes
Using multiple database management systems within a single application
Persisting data in a single database system to maintain consistency
Utilizing multiple programming languages for database interaction

questions.question: Which challenge is commonly associated with maintaining polyglot persistence?
Which query optimization technique involves restructuring SQL queries to produce the same result more efficiently?
How does query parallelization contribute to database optimization?
options: By executing multiple queries in sequence to reduce resource consumption
By executing multiple queries concurrently to improve performance
By optimizing query execution plans for complex queries
By limiting the number of concurrent database connections

questions.question: What is schema-on-read in the context of data lakes?
options: Data is stored in a structured format before being read by applications
Data is stored without a predefined schema and is structured at the time of analysis
Data is transformed into a standard schema before being stored in the data lake
Data is encrypted and secured before being read by applications

questions.question: What is the purpose of database auditing?
options: To encrypt data stored in the database
To monitor and record database activity for security and compliance purposes
To restrict access to certain database objects based on user roles
To authenticate users before allowing them to access the database

questions.question: Which property of BASE emphasizes that a system continues to operate even in the presence of partial failures?
Which aspect of BASE ensures that system performance remains stable as load increases?
What does data lineage tracing involve?
options: Tracing the movement and transformation of data across systems and processes
Auditing user access to sensitive data
Validating data consistency within a database

questions.question: What is one of the biggest challenges associated with data migration?
options: Implementing encryption for data in transit
Managing access controls within the database

questions.question: What is a weak entity in an ER model?
options: An entity that does not have a primary key
An entity that relies on another entity for identification
An entity with a high degree of cardinality
An entity that contains only derived attributes
